source.title: Better prediction of functional effects for sequence variants

initial_request.research_problem: Improving prediction of functional effects of sequence variants (effect vs. neutral) over previous SNAP method, motivated by personalized health needs and challenges of limited/imbalanced training data and sequence similarity.
initial_request.known_context: Existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change. SNAP predecessor exists.
quantifiable_goals: Achieve high AUC on ALL dataset

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need to distinguish functional from neutral sequence variants for personalized health; existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods do not predict molecular function change directly; SNAP predecessor may be improved.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A neural network classifier trained on diverse variant data and many features can better distinguish effect from neutral variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design SNAP2 as a neural network classifier to predict molecular function change, not disease.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 designed to improve over SNAP by screening many features and refining datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to compile training data for the classifier.

evidence.quote_or_span: Elucidating the effects of naturally occurring genetic variation is one of the major challenges for personalized health and personalized medicine.

In contrast, the current version of our SNAP (Screening for Non-Acceptable Polymorphisms) method, SNAP2, does not predict the variant effect as “disease or not” but rather as “change of molecular function or not”.

Here, we introduce SNAP2, a novel neural network based classifier that improves over the state-of-the-art in distinguishing between effect and neutral variants. Our method's improved performance results from screening many potentially relevant protein features and from refining our development data sets.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need diverse and balanced training data with careful class assignment.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PMD, EC, disease variants, and protein-specific studies will provide sufficient training data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compile training data from PMD, EC, OMIM, HumVar, LacI, and HIV-1 protease; assign effect/neutral classes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Compiled ALL dataset with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need cross-validation strategy to avoid overfitting due to sequence similarity.

evidence.quote_or_span: PMD annotations with ‘no change’ (‘=’) qualification (function equivalent to wild-type) were assigned to the ‘neutral’ class, while variants with any level of increase (‘+’, ‘++’, ‘+++’) or decrease (‘-’, ‘- -’, ‘- - -’) in function were assigned to the ‘effect’ class.
First, $PMD + EC + Disease$ were compiled into one comprehensive set termed ALL with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.
We extracted 22,858 human disease-associated variants in 3,537 proteins from OMIM [24] and HumVar [25].
This approach identified 51,817 variants (neutral: 13,638, effect: 38,179) in 4,061 proteins.
We also included data from comprehensive studies of particular proteins, namely LacI repressor from Escherichia coli [31] (4,041 variants) and the HIV-1 protease [32] (336 variants).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training data compiled; risk of overfitting due to sequence similarity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Proteins with similar sequences may bias cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Clustering proteins by sequence similarity and splitting clusters into folds will provide unbiased cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use PSI-BLAST (E<10^-3) single linkage clustering; 10-fold cross-validation with random cluster split.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cross-validation strategy established to avoid overfitting.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to define input features for the neural network.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also split the PMD data into two subsets: one containing only human mutations ( $PMD\_HUMAN$ ; 9,657 variants in 678 human sequences) and one consisting of all others ( $PMD\_NON-HUMAN$ ; 42,160 variants in 3,383 sequences).
First, $PMD + EC + Disease$ were compiled into one comprehensive set termed ALL with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cross-validation strategy defined; need input features.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which features best distinguish effect from neutral variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolutionary information, biophysical properties, structural features, and new annotations will improve prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Include PSIC profiles, AAindex, binding residues, disorder, co-evolution, Pfam/PROSITE annotations; use sliding windows up to 21 residues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Feature set defined including evolutionary, structural, and biophysical features.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to select optimal features and architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: features: global features (amino acid composition, secondary structure and solvent accessibility composition, and protein length), PSI-BLAST [27] profiles and deltas, PSIC [12] profiles and deltas (differences between mutant and wild-type residue annotations; see Methods for details), residue flexibility, sequence and variant profiles, disorder, secondary structure and relative solvent accessibility and their deltas, physicochemical properties (charge, hydrophobicity, volume, and their deltas), contact potential pr

network included the following features: global features (amino acid composition, secondary structure and solvent accessibility composition, and protein length), PSI-BLAST [27] profiles and deltas, PSIC [12] profiles and deltas (differences between mutant and wild-type residue annotations; see Methods for details), residue flexibility, sequence and variant profiles, disorder, secondary structure and relative solvent accessibility and their deltas, physicochemical properties (charge, hydrophobicity, volume, and thei
each feature could be selected in a window of w = 1,5,9,13,17, or 21 consecutive residues around the observed variant at the center of the window.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Feature set defined; need to select optimal subset and architecture.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Not all features may be useful; architecture parameters need tuning.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Greedy bottom-up feature selection with backward elimination and heuristic architecture optimization will yield best performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform greedy feature selection per network using AUC on cross-training set; optimize hidden nodes, learning rate, momentum.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Feature selection and architecture optimization completed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate final model performance via cross-validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: As an exhaustive screening of the entire parameter space was not intended, we heuristically selected parameter combinations for optimization: learning rate 0.005-0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model trained with selected features and architecture.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2 will achieve higher accuracy and AUC than SNAP, PolyPhen-2, and SIFT.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Cross-validate SNAP2 on ALL dataset and compare to other methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 achieved 83% two-state accuracy, AUC 0.905, outperforming SNAP (0.880), PolyPhen-2 (0.853), SIFT (0.838).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze performance by organism and variant difficulty.

evidence.quote_or_span: Cross-validated on >100k experimentally annotated variants, SNAP2 significantly outperformed other methods, attaining a two-state accuracy (effect/neutral) of 83%.
SNAP2 (dark blue, AUC = 0.905) outperforms its predecessor SNAP (light blue, AUC = 0.880), PolyPhen-2 (orange, AUC = 0.853) and SIFT (green, AUC = 0.838)

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does performance vary between human and non-human variants? Are neutral variants harder?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Performance may differ by organism; neutral variants are harder to predict.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze performance on PMD_HUMAN and PMD_NON-HUMAN subsets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 slightly better on non-human; neutral variants harder across methods.

evidence.quote_or_span: The F-measure for predicting effect ( $F_{effect}$ , Eqn. 3), the two state-accuracy (Q2, Eqn. 4), and the Matthew's correlation coefficient (MCC, Eqn. 5) were slightly higher for SNAP2 when tested on the non-human than on the human set
All methods performed significantly worse for neutral than for effect variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Performance varies by organism; neutral variants harder.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does SNAP2 perform on difficult variants where methods disagree?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2's use of non-alignment features like co-evolution will improve difficult case prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze performance on ALL-difficult subset and examine feature contributions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 correctly predicted 69% of difficult cases vs SNAP 53%, SIFT 41%; improvement partly from non-alignment features.

evidence.quote_or_span: SNAP2 outperformed others on the difficult cases, correctly predicting 69%, as compared to SNAP with 53% and SIFT with 41%
one important source of SNAP2 improvement for difficult cases originates from its use on various pieces of information, not just alignments
For these difficult cases, our new method SNAP2 significantly outperformed SNAP (set ALL-difficult: Q2(snap2) = 69%, Q2(snap) = 53%) and SIFT (Q2(sift) = 41%).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNAP2 requires alignments; 10-20% of proteins are orphans.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to predict for orphan proteins without alignments.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A version using global features and effect probability matrix can achieve reasonable accuracy from sequence alone.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2_noali achieves Q2 around 68% from sequence alone for 6.5 million orphan families.

evidence.quote_or_span: This feature reduces prediction runtime by over two orders of magnitude, enables cross-genome comparisons, and renders our new method as the best solution for the 10-20% of sequence orphans.
we developed a classifier that still achieves a two-state accuracy Q2 around 68% from sequence alone even for these 6.5 million orphan families.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need independent validation of SNAP2 performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2 will perform well on LacI and HIV-1 protease datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SNAP2 on independent LacI (4,041 variants) and HIV-1 protease (336 variants) datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Q2 = 78% for LacI, Q2 = 72% for HIV-1 protease.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations in comparison and data.

evidence.quote_or_span: they provide independent evidence to estimate the performance of SNAP2: Q2 = 78% for 4,041 LacI variants and Q2 = 72% for 336 HIV-1 variants.
However, they provide independent evidence to estimate the performance of SNAP2: Q2 = 78% for 4,041 LacI variants and Q2 = 72% for 336 HIV-1 variants.
We also included data from comprehensive studies of particular proteins, namely LacI repressor from Escherichia coli [31] (4,041 variants) and the HIV-1 protease [32] (336 variants).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Direct comparison complicated by training overlaps and threshold optimization; PolyPhen-2 performance may be overestimated on human data.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Comparison issues may affect perceived performance differences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitations in comparison and data quality.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Comparison complicated; PolyPhen-2 overestimated on human data.

evidence.quote_or_span: the direct comparison is complicated due to a variety of issues
PolyPhen-2 is optimized on human variants that account for only 25% of our ALL data. For these, we over-estimate PolyPhen-2's performance

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limited improvement despite many changes; possible performance plateau; EC data inconsistent but necessary.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Performance plateau or data issues may limit further gains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge performance plateau and data inconsistency.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Performance plateau suspected; EC data inconsistent but needed for peak performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: we made so many so important changes to SNAP that we were surprised not to improve more.
Was this because prediction performance has reached a plateau, i.e. have we reached the limits for a method using only sequence information as input?
we observed that our EC data was inconsistent but that we fared worse by leaving it out.

validation_methods: Independent validation on LacI and HIV-1 protease datasets
success_verification.final_conclusion: SNAP2 significantly outperforms SNAP, PolyPhen-2, and SIFT on variant effect prediction, with 83% accuracy and AUC 0.905. SNAP2_noali provides reasonable accuracy for orphan proteins. Limitations include comparison issues and potential performance plateau.
limitations: Direct comparison complicated by training overlaps and threshold optimization
PolyPhen-2 performance overestimated on human data
Limited improvement despite many changes; possible performance plateau
EC data inconsistent but necessary for peak performance
